From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Creation and selling of music

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/MCI_equipped_Studiod.jpg/250px-MCI_equipped_Studiod.jpg)](/wiki/File:MCI_equipped_Studiod.jpg)

Musicians working in a [recording studio](/wiki/Recording_studio "Recording studio")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Crowd_watching_The_Proclaimers%2C_Towersey_2018.jpg/250px-Crowd_watching_The_Proclaimers%2C_Towersey_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crowd_watching_The_Proclaimers,_Towersey_2018.jpg)

An audience watching a [concert](/wiki/Concert "Concert")

The **music industry** are individuals and organizations that earn money by [writing songs](/wiki/Songwriter "Songwriter") and [musical compositions](/wiki/Musical_composition "Musical composition"), creating and selling [recorded music](/wiki/Sound_recording_and_reproduction "Sound recording and reproduction") and [sheet music](/wiki/Sheet_music "Sheet music"), presenting [concerts](/wiki/Live_music "Live music"), as well as the organizations that aid, train, represent and supply [music](/wiki/Music "Music") creators. Among the many individuals and organizations that operate in the industry are: the songwriters and [composers](/wiki/Composer "Composer") who write songs and musical compositions; the [singers](/wiki/Singer "Singer"), [musicians](/wiki/Musician "Musician"), [conductors](/wiki/Conducting "Conducting"), and [bandleaders](/wiki/Bandleader "Bandleader") who perform the music; the [record labels](/wiki/Record_label "Record label"), [music publishers](/wiki/Music_publisher "Music publisher"), [recording studios](/wiki/Recording_studio "Recording studio"), [music producers](/wiki/Record_producer "Record producer"), [audio engineers](/wiki/Audio_engineer "Audio engineer"), [retail](/wiki/Record_shop "Record shop") and [digital music stores](/wiki/Digital_music_store "Digital music store"), and [performance rights organizations](/wiki/Performance_rights_organisation "Performance rights organisation") who create and sell recorded music and sheet music; and the [booking agents](/wiki/Talent_agent "Talent agent"), [promoters](/wiki/Promoter_(entertainment) "Promoter (entertainment)"), [music venues](/wiki/Music_venue "Music venue"), [road crew](/wiki/Road_crew "Road crew"), and audio engineers who help organize and sell concerts.

The industry also includes a range of professionals who assist singers and musicians with their music careers. These include [talent managers](/wiki/Talent_manager "Talent manager"), [artists and repertoire](/wiki/Artists_and_repertoire "Artists and repertoire") managers, [business managers](/wiki/Business_manager "Business manager"), [entertainment lawyers](/wiki/Entertainment_law "Entertainment law"); those who broadcast audio or video music content ([satellite](/wiki/Satellite_radio "Satellite radio"), [Internet radio stations](/wiki/Internet_radio "Internet radio"), [broadcast radio](/wiki/Radio_broadcasting "Radio broadcasting") and [TV stations](/wiki/Television_broadcaster "Television broadcaster")); [music journalists](/wiki/Music_journalist "Music journalist") and [music critics](/wiki/Music_criticism "Music criticism"); [DJs](/wiki/DJ "DJ"); [music educators and teachers](/wiki/Music_education "Music education"); manufacturers of [musical instruments](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument") and music equipment; as well as many others. In addition to the businesses and artists there are organizations that also play an important role, including musician's unions (e.g. [American Federation of Musicians](/wiki/American_Federation_of_Musicians "American Federation of Musicians")), not-for-profit performance-rights organizations (e.g. [American Society of Composers, Authors and Publishers](/wiki/American_Society_of_Composers,_Authors_and_Publishers "American Society of Composers, Authors and Publishers")) and other associations (e.g. [International Alliance for Women in Music](/wiki/International_Alliance_for_Women_in_Music "International Alliance for Women in Music"), a non-profit organization that advocates for [women composers and musicians](/wiki/Women_in_music "Women in music")).

The modern Western music industry emerged between the 1930s and 1950s, when [records](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record") replaced [sheet music](/wiki/Sheet_music "Sheet music") as the most important product in the music business. In the commercial world, "the recording industry"—a reference to [recording performances of songs and pieces](/wiki/Sound_recording "Sound recording") and selling the recordings–began to be used as a loose synonym for "the music industry". By the 2000s, a majority of the music market is controlled by three major corporate labels: the French-owned [Universal Music Group](/wiki/Universal_Music_Group "Universal Music Group"), the Japanese-owned [Sony Music Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Music_Entertainment "Sony Music Entertainment"),[[1]](#cite_note-sonybmg-1) and the American-owned [Warner Music Group](/wiki/Warner_Music_Group "Warner Music Group"). Labels outside of these three major labels are referred to as [independent labels](/wiki/Independent_label "Independent label") (or "indies"), such as [BMG](/wiki/BMG_Rights_Management "BMG Rights Management"), entertainment company [Concord](/wiki/Concord_(entertainment_company) "Concord (entertainment company)"), [Allied Artists Music Group](/wiki/Allied_Artists_Music_Group "Allied Artists Music Group"), [Reservoir Media](/wiki/Reservoir_Media "Reservoir Media"), [Secretly Group](/wiki/Secretly_Group "Secretly Group"), [Big Machine Label Group](/wiki/Big_Machine_Records "Big Machine Records"), [PIAS Group](/wiki/PIAS_Group "PIAS Group"), [Beggars Group](/wiki/Beggars_Group "Beggars Group"), [Sub Pop](/wiki/Sub_Pop "Sub Pop"), [Domino Recording Company](/wiki/Domino_Recording_Company "Domino Recording Company"), [Glassnote Records](/wiki/Glassnote_Records "Glassnote Records"), [Ninja Tune](/wiki/Ninja_Tune "Ninja Tune"), [Empire Distribution](/wiki/Empire_Distribution "Empire Distribution"), & [ECR Music Group](/wiki/ECR_Music_Group "ECR Music Group"). The largest portion of the live music market for concerts and tours is controlled by [Live Nation](/wiki/Live_Nation "Live Nation"), the largest promoter and [music venue](/wiki/Music_venue "Music venue") owner. Live Nation is a former subsidiary of [iHeartMedia Inc](/wiki/IHeartMedia "IHeartMedia"), which is the largest owner of radio stations in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").

In the first decades of the 2000s, the music industry underwent drastic changes with the advent of widespread digital distribution of music via the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") (which includes both illegal [file sharing](/wiki/File_sharing "File sharing") of songs and legal music purchases in [online music stores](/wiki/Online_music_store "Online music store")). A conspicuous indicator of these changes is total music sales: since the year 2000, sales of recorded music have dropped off substantially,[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-CNN-3) while, in contrast, [live music](/wiki/Live_music "Live music") has increased in importance.[[4]](#cite_note-Live_Nation-4) In 2011, the largest recorded music retailer in the world was now a digital, Internet-based platform operated by a computer company: [Apple Inc.](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.")'s online [iTunes Store](/wiki/ITunes_Store "ITunes Store").[[5]](#cite_note-5) Since 2011, the music industry has seen consistent sales growth with streaming now generating more revenue per year than digital downloads. [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify"), [Apple Music](/wiki/Apple_Music "Apple Music"), and [Amazon Music](/wiki/Amazon_Music "Amazon Music") are the largest streaming services by subscriber count.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Business structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_industry&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Business structure")]

The main branches of the music industry are the [live music](/wiki/Concert "Concert") industry, the [recording industry](/wiki/Record_sales "Record sales"), and all the companies that train, support, supply and represent musicians.

The recording industry produces three separate products: [compositions](/wiki/Musical_composition "Musical composition") (songs, pieces, lyrics), [recordings](/wiki/Sound_recording_and_reproduction "Sound recording and reproduction") (audio and video) and media (such as [CDs](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc") or [MP3s](/wiki/MP3 "MP3"), and [DVDs](/wiki/DVD "DVD")). These are each a type of [property](/wiki/Property "Property"): typically, compositions are owned by composers, recordings by record companies, and media by consumers. There may be many recordings of a single composition and a single recording will typically be distributed via many media. For example, the song "[My Way](/wiki/My_Way "My Way")" is owned by its composers, [Paul Anka](/wiki/Paul_Anka "Paul Anka") and [Claude François](/wiki/Claude_Fran%C3%A7ois "Claude François"), [Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra")'s recording of "My Way" is owned by [Capitol Records](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records"), [Sid Vicious](/wiki/Sid_Vicious "Sid Vicious")'s recording of "My Way" is owned by [Virgin Records](/wiki/Virgin_Records "Virgin Records"), and the millions of CDs and vinyl records that can play these recordings are owned by millions of individual consumers.

### Compositions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_industry&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Compositions")]

[Songs](/wiki/Song "Song"), instrumental pieces and other musical compositions are created by [songwriters](/wiki/Songwriter "Songwriter") or [composers](/wiki/Composer "Composer") and are originally owned by the composer, although they may be [sold](/wiki/Sales "Sales") or the rights may be otherwise assigned. For example, in the case of [work for hire](/wiki/Work_for_hire "Work for hire"), the composition is owned immediately by another party. Traditionally, the copyright owner [licenses](/wiki/Licensed "Licensed") or "assigns" some of their rights to [publishing companies](/wiki/Publishing_companies "Publishing companies"), by means of a [publishing contract](/wiki/Publishing_contract "Publishing contract"). The publishing company, or a collection society operating on behalf of many such publishers, songwriters and composers, collects fees, known as "[publishing royalties](/wiki/Music_royalties "Music royalties")", when the composition is used.

A portion of the royalties are paid by the publishing company to the copyright owner, depending on the terms of the contract. [Sheet music](/wiki/Sheet_music "Sheet music") provides an income stream that is paid exclusively to the composers and their publishing company. Typically, although not universally, the publishing company will provide the owner with an [advance](/wiki/Advance_payment "Advance payment") against future earnings when the publishing contract is signed. A publishing company will also promote the compositions, such as by acquiring song "placements" on [television](/wiki/Television "Television") or in [films](/wiki/Film "Film").

### Recordings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_industry&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Recordings")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Sound_Gobo.jpg/250px-Sound_Gobo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sound_Gobo.jpg)

A musician in a recording studio

Recordings are created by [recording artists](/wiki/Recording_artist "Recording artist"), which includes [singers](/wiki/Singer "Singer"), [musicians](/wiki/Musician "Musician") (including [session musicians](/wiki/Session_musician "Session musician")) and [musical ensembles](/wiki/Musical_ensemble "Musical ensemble") (e.g. [backing bands](/wiki/Backing_band "Backing band"), [rhythm sections](/wiki/Rhythm_section "Rhythm section"), [orchestras](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra"), etc.) usually with the assistance and guidance from [record producers](/wiki/Record_producer "Record producer") and [audio engineers](/wiki/Audio_engineer "Audio engineer"). They were traditionally made in [recording studios](/wiki/Recording_studio "Recording studio") (which are rented for a daily or hourly rate) in a [recording session](/wiki/Recording_session "Recording session"). In the 21st century, advances in [digital recording](/wiki/Digital_recording "Digital recording") technology have allowed many producers and artists to create "[home studios](/wiki/Home_studio "Home studio")" using high-end computers and digital recording programs like [Pro Tools](/wiki/Pro_Tools "Pro Tools"), bypassing the traditional role of the commercial recording studio.

The [record producer](/wiki/Record_producer "Record producer") oversees all aspects of the recording, making many of the logistic, financial and artistic decisions in cooperation with the artists. The record producer has a range of different responsibilities including choosing material and/or working with the composers, hiring session musicians, helping to [arrange](/wiki/Musical_arrangement "Musical arrangement") the songs, overseeing the musician performances, and directing the audio engineer during recording and mixing to get the best sound.

[Audio engineers](/wiki/Audio_engineer "Audio engineer"), including [recording](/wiki/Recording_engineer "Recording engineer"), [mixing](/wiki/Mixing_engineer "Mixing engineer") and [mastering engineers](/wiki/Mastering_engineer "Mastering engineer"), are responsible for ensuring good audio quality during the recording. They select and set up microphones and use [effects units](/wiki/Effects_unit "Effects unit") and [mixing consoles](/wiki/Mixing_console "Mixing console") to adjust the sound and level of the music. A recording session may also require the services of an [arranger](/wiki/Arranger "Arranger"), [orchestrator](/wiki/Orchestrator "Orchestrator"), [studio musicians](/wiki/Studio_musician "Studio musician"), [session musicians](/wiki/Session_musician "Session musician"), [vocal coaches](/wiki/Vocal_coach "Vocal coach"), or even a discreetly hired [ghostwriter to help with the lyrics or songwriting](/wiki/Ghostwriter "Ghostwriter").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Ben_Feggans.jpg/250px-Ben_Feggans.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ben_Feggans.jpg)

A studio engineer working with an [audio mixer](/wiki/Audio_mixer "Audio mixer") in a recording studio

Recordings are traditionally owned by [record companies](/wiki/Record_companies "Record companies"). Some artists own their own record companies (e.g. [Ani DiFranco](/wiki/Ani_DiFranco "Ani DiFranco")). A [recording contract](/wiki/Recording_contract "Recording contract") specifies the business relationship between a recording artist and the record company. In a traditional contract, the company provides an advance to the artist who agrees to make a recording that will be owned by the company. The [A&R](/wiki/A%26R "A&R") department of a record company is responsible for finding new talent and overseeing the recording process. The company pays for the recording costs and the cost of [promoting](/wiki/Radio_promotion "Radio promotion") and [marketing](/wiki/Marketing "Marketing") the record.

For physical media, such as [CDs](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc"), the company also pays to [manufacture](/wiki/Manufacture "Manufacture") and [distribute](/wiki/Distribution_(business) "Distribution (business)") the physical recordings. Smaller record companies (known as "[indies](/wiki/Independent_record_label "Independent record label")") will form business relationships with other companies to handle many of these tasks. The record company pays the recording artist a portion of the income from the sale of the recordings, also known as a "royalty", but this is distinct from the publishing royalties described above. This portion is similar to a percentage, but may be limited or expanded by a number of factors (such as free goods, recoupable expenses, bonuses, etc.) that are specified by the record contract. [Session musicians](/wiki/Session_musician "Session musician") and [orchestra](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra") members (as well as a few recording artists in special markets) are under contract to provide [work for hire](/wiki/Work_for_hire "Work for hire"); they are typically only paid one-time fees or regular wages for their services, rather than ongoing royalties.

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_industry&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Media")]

Physical media, such as CDs or vinyl records, are sold by music [retailers](/wiki/Retail "Retail") and are owned by the consumers after they buy them. Buyers do not typically have the right to make digital copies from CDs or other media they buy, or rent or lease the CDs, because they do not own the recording on the CD, they only own the individual physical CD. A music distributor delivers crates of the packaged physical media from the manufacturer to the retailer and maintains commercial relationships with retailers and record companies. The music retailer pays the distributor, who in turn pays the [record company](/wiki/Record_company "Record company") for the recordings. The record company pays [mechanical royalties](/wiki/Mechanical_royalties#Mechanical_royalties "Mechanical royalties") to the publisher and composer via a collection society. The record company then pays royalties, if contractually obligated, to the recording artist.

When music is digitally downloaded or [streamed](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media"), there is no physical media other than the consumer's computer memory on his or her portable media player or laptop. For this reason, artists such as Taylor Swift, Paul McCartney, Kings of Leon, and others have called for legal changes that would deny social media the right to stream their music without paying them royalties.[[7]](#cite_note-Knopper2009-7) In the digital and online music market of the 2000s, the distributor becomes optional.

Large online shops may pay the labels directly, but digital distributors do exist to provide distribution services for vendors large and small. When purchasing digital downloads or listening to music streaming, the consumer may be required to agree to record company and vendor licensing terms beyond those which are inherent in [copyright](/wiki/Copyright "Copyright"); for example, some services may allow consumers to freely share the recording, but others may restrict the user to storing the music on a specific number of hard drives or devices.

### Broadcast, soundtrack and streaming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_industry&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Broadcast, soundtrack and streaming")]

When a recording is broadcast (either on radio or by a [background music](/wiki/Background_music "Background music") service such as [Muzak](/wiki/Muzak_Holdings "Muzak Holdings")), [performance rights organisations](/wiki/Performance_rights_organisation "Performance rights organisation") (such as the [ASCAP](/wiki/American_Society_of_Composers,_Authors_and_Publishers "American Society of Composers, Authors and Publishers") and [BMI](/wiki/Broadcast_Music_Incorporated "Broadcast Music Incorporated") in the US, SOCAN in Canada, or [MCPS](/wiki/Mechanical-Copyright_Protection_Society "Mechanical-Copyright Protection Society") and [PRS](/wiki/PRS_for_Music "PRS for Music") in the UK), collect a third type of royalty known as a performance royalty, which is paid to songwriters, composers and recording artists. This royalty is typically much smaller than publishing or [mechanical royalties](/wiki/Mechanical_royalties#Mechanical_royalties "Mechanical royalties"). Within the past decade, more than "15 to 30 percent" of tracks on streaming services are unidentified with a specific artist. Jeff Price says "Audiam, an online music streaming service, has made over several hundred thousand dollars in the past year from collecting royalties from online streaming.[[8]](#cite_note-billboard.com-8)

According to Ken Levitan, manager from Kings of Leon, Cheap Trick and others, "Youtube has become radio for kids". Because of the overuse of YouTube and offline streaming, album sales have fallen by 60 percent in the past few years.[[7]](#cite_note-Knopper2009-7) When recordings are used in [television](/wiki/Television "Television") and [film](/wiki/Film "Film"), the composer and their publishing company are typically paid through a [synchronization license](/wiki/Synchronization_rights "Synchronization rights"). In the 2000s, online subscription services (such as [Rhapsody](/wiki/Rhapsody_(online_music_service) "Rhapsody (online music service)")) also provide an income stream directly to record companies, and through them, to artists, contracts permitting.

### Live music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_industry&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Live music")]

A [promoter](/wiki/Promoter_(entertainment) "Promoter (entertainment)") brings together a performing artist and a [venue](/wiki/Music_venue "Music venue") owner and arranges contracts. A [booking agency](/wiki/Booking_agency "Booking agency") represents the artist to promoters, makes deals and books performances. Consumers usually buy tickets either from the venue or from a ticket distribution service such as [Ticketmaster](/wiki/Ticketmaster "Ticketmaster"). In the US, [Live Nation](/wiki/Live_Nation "Live Nation") is the dominant company in all of these roles: they own most of the large venues in the US, they are the largest promoter, and they own Ticketmaster. Choices about where and when to tour are decided by the [artist's management](/wiki/Artist_manager "Artist manager") and the artist, sometimes in consultation with the [record company](/wiki/Record_company "Record company"). Record companies may finance a tour in the hopes that it will help promote the sale of recordings. In the 21st century, it has become more common to release recordings to promote ticket sales for live shows, rather than book tours to promote the sales of recordings.

Major, successful artists will usually employ a [road crew](/wiki/Road_crew "Road crew"): a semi-permanent touring organization that travels with the artist during concert series. The road crew is headed by a [tour manager](/wiki/Tour_manager "Tour manager"). Crew members provides [stage lighting](/wiki/Stage_lighting "Stage lighting"), [live sound reinforcement](/wiki/Audio_engineering "Audio engineering"), [musical instrument maintenance](/wiki/Guitar_technician "Guitar technician") and [transportation](/wiki/Trucking "Trucking"). On large tours, the road crew may also include an accountant, stage manager, bodyguard, hairdressers, makeup artists and [catering](/wiki/Catering "Catering") staff. Local crews are typically hired to help move equipment on and off stage. On a small tour with less financial backing, all of these jobs may be handled by just a few roadies or by the musicians themselves. Bands signed with small "indie" labels and bands in genres such as [hardcore punk](/wiki/Hardcore_punk "Hardcore punk") are more likely to do tours without a road crew, or with minimal support.

### Artist management, representation and staff

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_industry&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Artist management, representation and staff")]

Artists such as singers and musicians may hire several people from other fields to assist them with their career. The [artist manager](/wiki/Artist_manager "Artist manager") oversees all aspects of an artist's career in exchange for a percentage of the artist's income. An [entertainment lawyer](/wiki/Entertainment_law "Entertainment law") assists them with the details of their contracts with record companies and other deals. A [business manager](/wiki/Business_manager "Business manager") handles financial transactions, taxes, and bookkeeping. Unions, such as [AFTRA](/wiki/AFTRA "AFTRA") and the [American Federation of Musicians](/wiki/American_Federation_of_Musicians "American Federation of Musicians") in the U.S. provide [health insurance](/wiki/Health_insurance "Health insurance") and instrument insurance for musicians.[[9]](#cite_note-info.dcc-studios.com-9)

A successful artist functions in the market as a [brand](/wiki/Brand "Brand") and, as such, they may derive income from many other streams, such as [merchandise](/wiki/Merchandise "Merchandise"), personal endorsements, appearances (without performing) at events or Internet-based services.[[9]](#cite_note-info.dcc-studios.com-9) These are typically overseen by the artist's [manager](/wiki/Artist_manager "Artist manager") and take the form of relationships between the artist and companies that specialize in these products. Singers may also hire a [vocal coach](/wiki/Vocal_coach "Vocal coach"), [dance instructor](/wiki/Dance_teacher "Dance teacher"), [acting coach](/wiki/Drama_teacher "Drama teacher"), [personal trainer](/wiki/Personal_trainer "Personal trainer") or [life coach](/wiki/Life_coach "Life coach") to help them.

### Emerging business models

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_industry&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Emerging business models")]

In the 2000s, traditional lines that once divided singers, instrumentalists, publishers, record companies, distributors, retail and consumer electronics have become blurred or erased. Artists may record in a [home studio](/wiki/Home_studio "Home studio") using a high-end [laptop](/wiki/Laptop "Laptop") and a digital recording program such as [Pro Tools](/wiki/Pro_Tools "Pro Tools") or use [Kickstarter](/wiki/Kickstarter "Kickstarter") to raise money for an expensive studio recording session without involving a record company. Artists may choose to exclusively promote and market themselves using only free online video sharing services such as [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") or using [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media") websites, bypassing traditional promotion and marketing by a record company.

In the 2000s, [consumer electronics](/wiki/Consumer_electronics "Consumer electronics") and [computer](/wiki/Computer "Computer") companies such as [Apple Computer](/wiki/Apple_Computer "Apple Computer") have become [digital music retailers](/wiki/Online_music_store "Online music store"). New digital music distribution technologies and the trends towards using [sampling](/wiki/Sampling_(music) "Sampling (music)") of older songs in new songs or blending different songs to create ["mashup"](/wiki/Mashup_(music) "Mashup (music)") recordings have also forced both governments and the music industry to re-examine the definitions of [intellectual property](/wiki/Intellectual_property "Intellectual property") and the rights of all the parties involved. Also compounding the issue of defining copyright boundaries is the fact that the definition of "royalty" and "copyright" varies from country to country and region to region, which changes the terms of some of these business relationships.

After 15 or so years of the Internet economy, the digital music industry platforms like [iTunes](/wiki/ITunes "ITunes"), [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify"), and [Google Play](/wiki/Google_Play_Music "Google Play Music") are major improvements over the early illegal file sharing days. However, the multitude of service offerings and revenue models make it difficult to understand the true value of each and what they can deliver for musicians and music companies. As well, there are major transparency problems throughout the music industry caused by outdated technology. With the emerging of new business models as streaming platforms, and online music services, a large amount of data is processed.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Access to [big data](/wiki/Big_data "Big data") may increase transparency in the industry.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## History of printed music and recorded music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_industry&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: History of printed music and recorded music")]

### Early history: Printed music in Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_industry&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Early history: Printed music in Europe")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/CordierColor.jpg/330px-CordierColor.jpg)](/wiki/File:CordierColor.jpg)

Prior to the invention of the [printing press](/wiki/Printing_press "Printing press"), the only way to copy [sheet music](/wiki/Sheet_music "Sheet music") was by hand, a costly and time-consuming process. Pictured is the hand-written music manuscript for a French [Ars subtilior](/wiki/Ars_subtilior "Ars subtilior") chanson (song) from the late 1300s about love, entitled *Belle, bonne, sage*, by Baude Cordier. The [music notation](/wiki/Music_notation "Music notation") is unusual in that it is written in a heart shape, with red notes indicating rhythmic [alterations](#Notational_characteristics).

Music publishing using machine-printed [sheet music](/wiki/Sheet_music "Sheet music") developed during the [Renaissance music](/wiki/Renaissance_music "Renaissance music") era in the mid-15th century. The development of music publication followed the evolution of [printing](/wiki/Printing "Printing") technologies that were first developed for printing regular [books](/wiki/Book "Book"). After the mid-15th century, mechanical techniques for printing sheet music were first developed. The earliest example, a set of liturgical chants, dates from about 1465, shortly after the [Gutenberg Bible](/wiki/Johannes_Gutenberg "Johannes Gutenberg") was printed.

Prior to this time, music had to be copied out by hand. To copy music notation by hand was a very costly, labor-intensive, and time-consuming process, so it was usually undertaken only by monks and priests seeking to preserve sacred music for the church. The few collections of secular (non-religious) music that are extant were commissioned and owned by wealthy aristocrats. Examples include the [Squarcialupi Codex](/wiki/Squarcialupi_Codex "Squarcialupi Codex") of Italian [Trecento](/wiki/Trecento "Trecento") music and the [Chantilly Codex](/wiki/Chantilly_Codex "Chantilly Codex") of French [Ars subtilior](/wiki/Ars_subtilior "Ars subtilior") music.

The use of printing enabled sheet music to be reproduced much more quickly and at a much lower cost than hand-copying music notation. This helped musical styles to spread to other cities and countries more quickly, and it also enabled music to be spread to more distant areas. Before the invention of music printing, a composer's music might only be known in the city she lived in and its surrounding towns, because only wealthy aristocrats would be able to afford to have hand copies made of her music.

With music printing, a composer's music could be printed and sold at a relatively low cost to purchasers from a wide geographic area. As sheet music of major composer's pieces and songs began to be printed and distributed in a wider area, this enabled composers and listeners to hear new styles and forms of music. A German composer could buy songs written by an Italian or English composer, and an Italian composer could buy pieces written by Dutch composers and learn how they wrote music. This led to more blending of musical styles from different countries and regions.

[Ottaviano Petrucci](/wiki/Ottaviano_Petrucci "Ottaviano Petrucci") was the pioneer of modern music printing (born in Fossombrone in 1466 – died in 1539 in Venice), a printer and publisher who was able to secure a twenty-year monopoly on printed music in Venice during the 16th century. [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice") was one of the major business and music centers during this period. His *Harmonice Musices Odhecaton*, a collection of chansons printed in 1501, is commonly misidentified as the first book of sheet music printed from movable type. That distinction belongs to the Roman printer Ulrich Han's *Missale Romanum* of 1476.

Nevertheless, Petrucci's later work was extraordinary for the complexity of his white mensural notation and the smallness of his font. He printed the first book of [polyphony](/wiki/Polyphony "Polyphony") (music with two or more independent melodic lines) using movable type. He also published numerous works by the most highly regarded composers of the Renaissance, including [Josquin des Prez](/wiki/Josquin_des_Prez "Josquin des Prez") and [Antoine Brumel](/wiki/Antoine_Brumel "Antoine Brumel"). He flourished by focusing on Flemish works, rather than Italian, as they were very popular throughout Europe during the [Renaissance music](/wiki/Renaissance_music "Renaissance music") era.

His printing shop used the triple-impression method, in which a sheet of paper was pressed three times. The first impression was the staff lines, the second the words, and the third the notes. This method produced very clean and readable results, although it was time-consuming and expensive.

Until the 18th century, the processes of formal composition and of the printing of music took place for the most part with the support of [patronage](/wiki/Patronage "Patronage") from [aristocracies](/wiki/Aristocracy_(class) "Aristocracy (class)") and [churches](/wiki/Religion "Religion"). In the mid-to-late 18th century, performers and composers such as [Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart](/wiki/Wolfgang_Amadeus_Mozart "Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart") began to seek more commercial opportunities to market their music and performances to the general public. After Mozart's death, his wife ([Constanze Weber](/wiki/Constanze_Weber "Constanze Weber")) continued the process of commercialization of his music through an unprecedented series of memorial concerts, selling his manuscripts, and collaborating with her second husband, [Georg Nissen](/wiki/Georg_Nissen "Georg Nissen"), on a biography of Mozart.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/AmazingGraceFamiliarStyle.png/250px-AmazingGraceFamiliarStyle.png)](/wiki/File:AmazingGraceFamiliarStyle.png)

An example of mechanically printed sheet music

In the 19th century, [sheet-music](/wiki/Sheet_music "Sheet music") publishers dominated the music industry. Before the invention of [sound recording](/wiki/Sound_recording "Sound recording") technologies, the main way for music lovers to hear new symphonies and [opera](/wiki/Opera "Opera") arias (songs) was to buy the sheet music (often arranged for piano or for a small chamber music group) and perform the music in a living room, using friends who were amateur musicians and singers. In the United States, the music industry arose in tandem with the rise of "[black face](/wiki/Black_face "Black face")" [minstrelsy](/wiki/Minstrel_show "Minstrel show"). Blackface is a form of theatrical makeup used predominantly by non-black performers to represent a [black person](/wiki/Black_people "Black people"). The practice gained popularity during the 19th century and contributed to the spread of negative [racial stereotypes](/wiki/Racial_stereotype "Racial stereotype") of African-American people.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In the late part of the century the group of music publishers and songwriters which dominated [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") in the United States became known as [Tin Pan Alley](/wiki/Tin_Pan_Alley "Tin Pan Alley"). The name originally referred to a specific place: West 28th Street between Fifth and Sixth Avenue in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), and a plaque (see [below](#plaque)) on the sidewalk on 28th Street between Broadway and Sixth commemorates it. The start of Tin Pan Alley is usually dated to about 1885, when several music publishers set up shop in the same district of Manhattan. The end of Tin Pan Alley is less clear-cut. Some date it to the start of the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") in the 1930s when the [phonograph](/wiki/Phonograph "Phonograph") and radio supplanted [sheet music](/wiki/Sheet_music "Sheet music") as the driving force of American popular music, while others consider Tin Pan Alley to have continued into the 1950s when earlier styles of American popular music were upstaged by the rise of [rock & roll](/wiki/Rock_%26_roll "Rock & roll").

### Advent of recorded music and radio broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_industry&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Advent of recorded music and radio broadcasting")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Frances_Densmore_recording_Mountain_Chief2.jpg/250px-Frances_Densmore_recording_Mountain_Chief2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frances_Densmore_recording_Mountain_Chief2.jpg)

[Frances Densmore](/wiki/Frances_Densmore "Frances Densmore") at the [Smithsonian Institution](/wiki/Smithsonian_Institution "Smithsonian Institution") in 1916 where she was recording [Blackfoot](/wiki/Blackfoot "Blackfoot") chief [Mountain Chief](/wiki/Mountain_Chief "Mountain Chief") for the [Bureau of American Ethnology](/wiki/Bureau_of_American_Ethnology "Bureau of American Ethnology"). In this picture, Mountain Chief is listening to a recording.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Donald_Manson_working_as_an_employee_of_the_Marconi_Company.jpg/250px-Donald_Manson_working_as_an_employee_of_the_Marconi_Company.jpg)](/wiki/File:Donald_Manson_working_as_an_employee_of_the_Marconi_Company.jpg)

A radio broadcasting system from 1906

At the dawn of the early 20th century, the development of [sound recording](/wiki/Sound_recording "Sound recording") began to function as a [disruptive technology](/wiki/Disruptive_technology "Disruptive technology") to the commercial interests which published sheet music. During the sheet music era, if a regular person wanted to hear popular new songs, he or she would buy the sheet music and play it at home on a piano, or learn the song at home while playing the [accompaniment](/wiki/Accompaniment "Accompaniment") part on piano or guitar. Commercially released [phonograph](/wiki/Phonograph "Phonograph") records of musical performances, which became available starting in the late 1880s, and later the onset of widespread [radio broadcasting](/wiki/Radio_broadcasting "Radio broadcasting"), starting in the 1920s, forever changed the way music was heard and listened to.

Opera houses, concert halls, and clubs continued to produce music and musicians and singers continued to perform live, but the power of radio allowed bands, ensembles and singers who had previously performed only in one region to become popular on a nationwide and sometimes even a worldwide scale. Moreover, whereas attendance at the top symphony and opera concerts was formerly restricted to high-income people in a pre-radio world, with [broadcast radio](/wiki/Broadcast_radio "Broadcast radio"), a much larger wider range of people, including lower and middle-income people could hear the best [orchestras](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra"), [big bands](/wiki/Big_band "Big band"), popular singers and opera shows.

The "record industry" eventually replaced the sheet music publishers as the music industry's largest force. A multitude of record labels came and went. Some noteworthy labels of the earlier decades include the [Columbia Records](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records"), Crystalate, [Decca Records](/wiki/Decca_Records "Decca Records"), Edison Bell, [The Gramophone Company](/wiki/The_Gramophone_Company "The Gramophone Company"), Invicta, Kalliope, [Pathé](/wiki/Path%C3%A9 "Pathé"), [Victor Talking Machine Company](/wiki/Victor_Talking_Machine_Company "Victor Talking Machine Company") and many others.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Many record companies died out as quickly as they had formed, and by the end of the 1980s, the "Big six" — [EMI](/wiki/EMI "EMI"), [CBS](/wiki/Sony_Music_Entertainment "Sony Music Entertainment"), [BMG](/wiki/Bertelsmann_Music_Group "Bertelsmann Music Group"), [PolyGram](/wiki/PolyGram "PolyGram"), [WEA](/wiki/Warner_Music_Group "Warner Music Group") and [MCA](/wiki/MCA_Records "MCA Records") — dominated the industry. [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony") bought [CBS Records](/wiki/CBS_Records_International "CBS Records International") in 1987 and changed its name to Sony Music in 1991. In mid-1998, PolyGram Music Group merged with MCA Music Entertainment creating what we now know as [Universal Music Group](/wiki/Universal_Music_Group "Universal Music Group").

Sony and BMG merged in 2004,[[15]](#cite_note-15) and Universal took over the majority of EMI's recorded music interests in 2012.[[16]](#cite_note-16) [EMI Music Publishing](/wiki/EMI_Music_Publishing "EMI Music Publishing"), also once part of the now defunct British conglomerate, is now co-owned by Sony as a subsidiary of [Sony/ATV Music Publishing](/wiki/Sony/ATV_Music_Publishing "Sony/ATV Music Publishing").[[17]](#cite_note-17) As in other industries, the record industry is characterised by many mergers and/or acquisitions, for the major companies as well as for middle sized business (recent example is given by the Belgium group [PIAS](/wiki/PIAS_Recordings "PIAS Recordings") and French group [Harmonia Mundi](/wiki/Harmonia_Mundi "Harmonia Mundi")).[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Genre-wise, music entrepreneurs expanded their industry models into areas like [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"), in which composition and performance had continued for centuries on an [ad hoc](/wiki/Ad_hoc "Ad hoc") self-supporting basis. Forming an [independent record label](/wiki/Independent_record_label "Independent record label"), or "indie" label, or signing to such a label continues to be a popular choice for up-and-coming musicians, especially in genres like [hardcore punk](/wiki/Hardcore_punk "Hardcore punk") and [extreme metal](/wiki/Extreme_metal "Extreme metal"), even though indies cannot offer the same financial backing of major labels. Some bands prefer to sign with an indie label, because these labels typically give performers more artistic freedom.

The logo for [Apple Inc.](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.")'s online [iTunes store](/wiki/ITunes_store "ITunes store"), which sells digital files of songs and musical pieces–along with a range of other content, such as digital files of [TV shows](/wiki/TV_show "TV show") and [movies](/wiki/Movie "Movie")

In the first decade of the 2000s, digitally downloaded and [streamed music](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") became more popular than buying physical recordings (e.g. [CDs](/wiki/CD "CD"), [records](/wiki/Gramophone_record "Gramophone record") and [tapes](/wiki/Compact_Cassette "Compact Cassette")). This gave consumers almost "friction-less" access to a wider variety of music than ever before, across multiple devices. At the same time, consumers spent less money on recorded music (both physically and digitally distributed) than they had in the 1990s.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Total "music-business" revenues in the U.S. dropped by half, from a high of $14.6 billion in 1999 to $6.3 billion in 2009.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Worldwide revenues for CDs, [vinyl](/wiki/Gramophone_record "Gramophone record"), cassettes and [digital downloads](/wiki/Music_download "Music download") fell from $36.9 billion in 2000[[21]](#cite_note-21) to $15.9 billion in 2010.[[22]](#cite_note-22) *[The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist")* and *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* reported that the downward trend was expected to continue for the foreseeable future.[[23]](#cite_note-NYT2008-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Economist2008-24) This dramatic decline in revenue has caused large-scale layoffs inside the industry, driven some more venerable retailers (such as [Tower Records](/wiki/Tower_Records_(music_retailer) "Tower Records (music retailer)")) out of business and forced record companies, record producers, studios, recording engineers and musicians to seek new [business models](/wiki/Business_model "Business model").[[7]](#cite_note-Knopper2009-7)

In response to the rise of widespread illegal [file sharing](/wiki/File_sharing "File sharing") of digital music-recordings, the record industry took aggressive legal action. In 2001 it succeeded in shutting down the popular music-website [Napster](/wiki/Napster "Napster"), and threatened legal action against thousands of individuals who participated in sharing music-song sound-files.[[7]](#cite_note-Knopper2009-7) However, this failed to slow the decline in music-recording revenue and proved a [public-relations](/wiki/Public-relations "Public-relations") disaster for the music industry.[[7]](#cite_note-Knopper2009-7)

Some academic studies have even suggested that downloads did not cause the decline in sales of recordings.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The 2008 British Music Rights survey[[26]](#cite_note-26) showed that 80% of people in Britain wanted a legal [peer-to-peer](/wiki/Peer-to-peer "Peer-to-peer") (P2P) file-sharing service, however only half of the respondents thought that the music's creators should be paid. The survey was consistent with the results of earlier research conducted in the United States, upon which the [Open Music Model](/wiki/Open_Music_Model "Open Music Model") was based.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Legal digital downloads became widely available with the debut of the Apple [iTunes Store](/wiki/ITunes_Store "ITunes Store") in 2003.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The popularity of music distribution over the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") has increased,[[29]](#cite_note-29) and by 2011 digital music sales topped physical sales of music.[[30]](#cite_note-30)
In 2008, Atlantic Records reports that digital sales have surpassed physical sales.[[23]](#cite_note-NYT2008-23)
However, as *The Economist* reported, "paid digital downloads grew rapidly, but did not begin to make up for the loss of revenue from CDs".[[24]](#cite_note-Economist2008-24)

After 2010, Internet-based services such as [Deezer](/wiki/Deezer "Deezer"), [Pandora](/wiki/Pandora_Radio "Pandora Radio"), [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify"), and [Apple's iTunes Radio](/wiki/ITunes_Radio "ITunes Radio") began to offer subscription-based "[pay to stream](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media")" services over the Internet. With streaming services, the user pays a subscription to a company for the right to listen to songs and other media from a library. Whereas with legal digital download services, the purchaser owns a digital copy of the song (which they can keep on their computer or on a digital media player), with streaming services, the user never downloads the song file or owns the song file. The subscriber can only listen to the song for as long as they continue to pay the streaming subscription. Once the user stops paying the subscription, they cannot listen to audio from the company's repositories anymore. Streaming services began to have a serious impact on the industry in 2014.

[Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify"), together with the [music-streaming](/wiki/Music_streaming "Music streaming") industry in general, faces some criticism from artists claiming they are not being fairly compensated for their work as downloaded-music sales decline and music-streaming increases. Unlike physical or download sales, which pay a fixed price per song or album, Spotify pays artists based on their "market share" (the number of streams for their songs as a proportion of total songs streamed on the service).[[31]](#cite_note-31) Spotify distributes approximately 70% to rights-holders, who will then pay artists based on their agreements. The variable, and (some say) inadequate nature of this compensation,[[32]](#cite_note-32) has led to criticism. Spotify reports paying on average US$0.006 to US$0.008 per stream. In response to concerns, Spotify claims that they are benefiting the music business by migrating "them away from piracy and less monetized platforms and allowing them to generate far greater royalties than before" by encouraging users to use their paid service.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)

The [Recording Industry Association of America](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America") (RIAA) revealed in its 2015 earnings report that streaming services were responsible for 34.3 percent of the year's U.S. recorded-music-industry revenue, growing 29 percent from the previous year and becoming the largest source of income, pulling in around $2.4 billion.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) US streaming revenue grew 57 percent to $1.6 billion in the first half of 2016 and accounted for almost half of industry sales.[[37]](#cite_note-37) This contrasts with the $14.6 billion in revenue that was received in 1999 by the U.S. music industry from the sale of CDs.

The turmoil in the recorded-music industry in the 2000s altered the twentieth-century balance between artists, record companies, promoters, retail music-stores and consumers. As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Music_industry&action=edit), [big-box stores](/wiki/Big-box_store "Big-box store") such as [Wal-Mart](/wiki/Wal-Mart "Wal-Mart") and [Best Buy](/wiki/Best_Buy "Best Buy") sell more records than music-only CD stores, which have ceased to function as a major player in the music industry. Music-performing artists now rely on [live performance](/wiki/Concert "Concert") and [merchandise](/wiki/Merchandise "Merchandise") sales (T-shirts, sweatshirts, etc.) for the majority of their income, which in turn has made them more dependent – like pre-20th-century musicians – on patrons, now exemplified by music promoters such as [Live Nation](/wiki/Live_Nation_(events_promoter) "Live Nation (events promoter)") (which dominates tour promotion and owns or manages a large number of [music venues](/wiki/Music_venue "Music venue")).[[4]](#cite_note-Live_Nation-4)

In order to benefit from all of an artist's income streams, record companies increasingly rely on the "[360 deal](/wiki/360_deal "360 deal")", a new business-relationship pioneered by [Robbie Williams](/wiki/Robbie_Williams "Robbie Williams") and EMI in 2007.[[38]](#cite_note-38)
At the other extreme, record companies can offer a simple manufacturing- and [distribution-deal](/wiki/Distribution_deal "Distribution deal"), which gives a higher percentage to the artist, but does not cover the expenses of marketing and promotion.

Companies like [Kickstarter](/wiki/Kickstarter "Kickstarter") help independent musicians produce their albums through [fans funding bands](/wiki/Crowdfunding "Crowdfunding") they want to listen to.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Many newer artists no longer see a [record deal](/wiki/Record_deal "Record deal") as an integral part of their [business plan](/wiki/Business_plan "Business plan") at all. Inexpensive recording-hardware and -software make it possible to record reasonable-quality music on a laptop in a bedroom and to distribute it over the Internet to a worldwide audience.[[40]](#cite_note-40) This has caused problems for recording studios, record producers and [audio engineers](/wiki/Audio_engineer "Audio engineer"): the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* reports that as many as half of the recording facilities in that city have failed.[[41]](#cite_note-41)
Changes in the music industry have given consumers access to a wider variety of music than ever before, at a price that gradually approaches zero.[[7]](#cite_note-Knopper2009-7) However, [consumer spending](/wiki/Consumer_spending "Consumer spending") on music-related software and hardware increased dramatically over the last decade,[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] providing a valuable new income-stream for [technology companies](/wiki/Technology_company "Technology company") such as [Apple Inc.](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") and [Pandora Radio](/wiki/Pandora_Radio "Pandora Radio").

### Consolidation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_industry&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Consolidation")]

World music market sales shares, according to IFPI (2005)

1. EMI (13.4%)
2. WMG (11.3%)
3. Sony BMG (21.5%)
4. UMG (25.5%)
5. Independent (28.4%)

Prior to December 1998, the industry was dominated by the "Big Six": Sony Music and BMG had not yet merged, and [PolyGram](/wiki/PolyGram "PolyGram") had not yet been absorbed into Universal Music Group. After the PolyGram-Universal merger, the 1998 market shares reflected a "Big Five", commanding 77.4% of the market, as follows, according to MEI World Report 2000:

* Universal Music Group — 21.1%
* Sony Music Entertainment — 17.4%
* EMI — 14.1%
* Warner Music Group — 13.4%
* BMG — 11.4%
* Independent labels combined — 22.6%

In 2004, the joint venture of Sony and BMG created the 'Big Four' at a time the global market was estimated at $30–40 billion.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Total annual unit sales (CDs, music videos, [MP3s](/wiki/MP3 "MP3")) in 2004 were 3 billion. Additionally, according to an [IFPI](/wiki/International_Federation_of_the_Phonographic_Industry "International Federation of the Phonographic Industry") report published in August 2005,[[44]](#cite_note-44) the big four accounted for 71.7% of retail music sales:

* Universal Music Group—25.5%
* Sony BMG Music Entertainment—21.5%
* EMI Group—13.4%
* Warner Music Group—11.3%
* Independent labels combined—28.3%

US music market shares, according to Nielsen SoundScan (2011)

1. EMI (9.62%)
2. WMG (19.1%)
3. SME (29.3%)
4. UMG (29.9%)
5. Independent (12.1%)

[Nielsen SoundScan](/wiki/Nielson_SoundScan "Nielson SoundScan") in their 2011 report noted that the "big four" controlled about 88% of the market:[[45]](#cite_note-45)

* [Universal Music Group](/wiki/Universal_Music_Group "Universal Music Group") (American based) — 29.85%
* [Sony Music Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Music_Entertainment "Sony Music Entertainment") (American based) — 29.29%
* [Warner Music Group](/wiki/Warner_Music_Group "Warner Music Group") (American based) — 19.13%
* EMI Group — 9.62%
* [Independent labels](/wiki/Category:Independent_record_labels "Category:Independent record labels") — 12.11%

After the absorption of EMI by Sony Music Entertainment and Universal Music Group in December 2011 the "big three" were created and on January 8, 2013, after the merger there were layoffs of forty workers from EMI. European regulators forced Universal Music to spin off EMI assets which became the Parlophone Label Group which was acquired by Warner Music Group.[[46]](#cite_note-46) [Nielsen SoundScan](/wiki/Nielson_SoundScan "Nielson SoundScan") issued a report in 2012, noting that these labels controlled 88.5% of the market, and further noted:[[47]](#cite_note-NielsenReport2012-47)

* [Universal Music Group](/wiki/Universal_Music_Group "Universal Music Group") (American based) which owns [EMI Music](/wiki/EMI_Music "EMI Music") — 32.41% + 6.78% of EMI Group
* [Sony Music Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Music_Entertainment "Sony Music Entertainment") (American based) which owns publishing arm of [EMI Group](/wiki/EMI "EMI") — 30.25%
* Warner Music Group— 19.15%
* Independent labels— 11.42%

Note: the IFPI and Nielsen Soundscan use different methodologies, which makes their figures difficult to compare casually, and impossible to compare scientifically.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

Market shares as of September 2018 are as follows:[[49]](#cite_note-IBISWorld_report_51221-49)

* Warner Music Group — 25.1%
* Universal Music Group — 24.3%
* Sony Corporation — 22.1%
* Other — 28.5%

The largest players in this industry own more than 100 subsidiary record labels or sublabels, each specializing in a certain market niche. Only the industry's most popular artists are signed directly to the major label. These companies account for more than half of US market share. However, this has fallen somewhat in recent years, as the new digital environment allows smaller labels to compete more effectively.[[49]](#cite_note-IBISWorld_report_51221-49)

#### 2003–2007

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_industry&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: 2003–2007")]

Approximately 21% of the [gross CD revenue](/wiki/Gross_revenue "Gross revenue") numbers in 2003 can be attributed to used CD sales.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This number grew to approximately 27% in 2007.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The growth is attributed to increasing on-line sales of used product by outlets such as Amazon.com, the growth of used music media is expected to continue to grow as the cost of digital downloads continues to rise.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The sale of used goods financially benefits the vendors and online marketplaces, but in the United States, the [first-sale doctrine](/wiki/First-sale_doctrine "First-sale doctrine") prevents copyright owners (record labels and publishers, generally) from "double dipping" through a levy on the sale of used music.

#### 2011

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_industry&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: 2011")]

In mid-2011, the RIAA trumpeted a sales increase of 5% over 2010, stating that "there's probably no one single reason" for the bump.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

#### 2012

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Music_industry&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: 2012")]

The Nielsen Company & Billboard's 2012 Industry Report shows overall music sales increased 3.1% over 2011. Digital sales caused this increase, with a Digital Album sales growth of 14.1% and Digital Track sales growth of 5.1%, whereas Physical Music sales decreased by 12.8% versus 2011. Despite the decrease, physical albums were still the dominant album format. Vinyl Record sales increased by 17.7% and Holiday Season Album sales decreased by 7.1%.[[47]](#cite_note-NielsenReport2012-47)
